description_long: This guard stone is a robust, rectangular block carved from stone, featuring two prominent sphinx-like figures with human faces and lion bodies positioned side by side at one end. The figures are detailed with stylized, segmented hair and serene facial expressions, while their bodies are adorned with incised lines to represent fur or feathers. The top surface is flat and circular, possibly intended to support another structure or serve as a base. The sides display the elongated bodies and legs of the sphinxes, with subtle relief work indicating musculature and paws. The rear of the object is plain, with the backs of the sphinxes and a seam running down the center, suggesting the piece may have been part of a pair or larger architectural ensemble. The stone surface is weathered, with minor chips and a generally matte, rough finish.
description: A stone guard stone featuring two detailed sphinx-like figures with human faces and lion bodies, supporting a flat, circular top. The surface is weathered and textured, with intricate carvings on the front and sides.
description_view_0: Side view showing the elongated bodies and legs of the two sphinxes, with detailed fur or feather carvings and a flat circular top.
description_view_1: Frontal view highlighting the two human-faced sphinxes side by side, their faces and paws clearly visible and symmetrically arranged.
description_view_2: Opposite side view mirroring view 0, showing the other profile of the sphinxes and the same circular top.
description_view_3: Rear view displaying the backs of the sphinxes, a central seam, and the plain, blocky structure of the object.
attribute_location: Typically found at the entrance to historical sites, temple precincts, or as a boundary marker at the edge of plazas or courtyards, often flanking pathways or driveways.
attribute_installation_orientation: Fully above ground, resting flush on a flat surface, sometimes partially embedded for stability.
attribute_wear_patterns: Visible surface weathering, minor chipping along edges and corners, and smoothing on the top and front faces likely caused by prolonged exposure to the elements, occasional contact with vehicles or foot traffic, and natural erosion.
